description: The provided image is unreadable for scientific purposes, as it displays only a publisher interface icon labeled 'Check for updates' with a bookmark symbol and lacks any material characterization data, plots, or micrographs. No caption is provided, and the pixel content does not reveal microstructures, diffraction patterns, spectra, or mechanical curves. Consequently, visual identification of the Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 alloy system, processing routes such as 35 h mechanical alloying or spark plasma sintering at 900 °C, and reported phases—including BCC, B2-type, γ-brass, or Fe5Si3 silicide precipitates—is impossible from the image alone. The accompanying text extraction summary reports that the study involves phase evolution, nanocrystalline grain structures, and strengthening mechanisms in a low-density high entropy steel, with characterization by XRD, SEM, EPMA, TEM, and compression testing; however, none of these elements are visible in the figure itself. Therefore, the image cannot be used to verify sample labels, scale bars, peak positions, or quantitative property trends, and its contribution to the paper's conclusions regarding microstructure–property relationships remains inaccessible from direct visual inspection.

Figure 1 presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high-entropy steel powder during mechanical alloying (0–35 h). Panel (a) displays full XRD patterns showing progressive dissolution of elemental Fe, Mn, Ni, Al, Si, and C peaks with milling time; by 25 h, a solid solution of BCC and γ-brass type phases emerges, and after 35 h, a multiphase structure dominated by BCC ferrite forms. Panel (b) provides an enlarged view (2θ = 49°–54°) highlighting the evolution of the BCC (110) peak and the emergence of a B2-type ordered phase (110) reflection. Panel (c) indexes the 35 h milled pattern, identifying peaks for BCC, γ-brass type {411}, {332}, {200}, {211}, B2-type (110), and residual Si (100). Panel (d) shows Rietveld refinement of the 35 h pattern using GSAS-II, confirming the coexistence of BCC, γ-brass type, B2-type, and trace Si phases with nanocrystallite sizes around 10–17 nm. The figure demonstrates that extended milling produces a nanostructured multiphase powder without detectable WC contamination, establishing the phase evolution pathway critical for subsequent spark plasma sintering.

Figure 2 presents SEM micrographs, EDS analysis, and particle size distribution histograms of Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high entropy steel powder subjected to mechanical milling (MM) for 10–35 h. Panels (a–d) show powder morphology evolution at 2 μm scale, where early milling stages (10 h and 20 h) exhibit random, bulky particles, whereas prolonged milling to 30 h and 35 h produces finer, more homogeneous powders. Histograms (f–i) quantify the systematic particle refinement, with average sizes decreasing from ~10 ± 2 μm (10 h), to ~6 ± 2 μm (20 h), ~5 ± 1.5 μm (30 h), and ~4 ± 1 μm (35 h). Panel (e) displays the EDS spectrum of 35 h milled powder, confirming peaks for C, Fe, Mn, Ni, Al, and Si. Panel (j) is a 10 μm scale SEM micrograph of the 35 h powder marked for point EDS and mapping; caption states light grey contrasts correspond to the BCC phase (Fe-rich) and dark grey contrasts to the γ-brass type structure (Mn-rich). Accompanying EDS elemental maps visualize the spatial distribution of Fe, Mn, Ni, Al, Si, and C. The figure collectively demonstrates that extended milling progressively reduces particle size and enhances morphological uniformity, while revealing a chemically heterogeneous dual-phase powder microstructure comprising Fe-rich BCC and Mn-rich γ-brass type phases prior to spark plasma sintering.

Figure 3 presents TEM characterization of the Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high-entropy steel powder after 35 h of mechanical alloying. Panel (a) shows a bright-field TEM image with a 20 nm scale bar, revealing a nanocrystalline microstructure composed of ultrafine grains formed during prolonged milling. Panel (b) displays the corresponding selected area electron diffraction pattern with a 10 1/nm scale bar, which exhibits distinct concentric polycrystalline rings. The caption and annotation identify these rings as originating from the BCC phase, with specific reflections from the (110), (200), and (211) planes, as marked by dashed blue lines. The polycrystalline ring pattern confirms the fine-grained nature of the alloy and establishes that the BCC crystal structure is the dominant phase in the 35 h milled powder. Collectively, these micrographs demonstrate that mechanical alloying for 35 h successfully produces a nanocrystalline BCC microstructure, corroborating the XRD findings of nanoscale grain formation and providing direct morphological and crystallographic evidence for the phase evolution during milling.

This figure presents a DTA thermogram of the 35 h mechanically alloyed Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 low-density high-entropy steel powder, recorded from 200 °C to 1000 °C with heat flow plotted in μV/mg. The curve displays two pronounced exothermic events, indicated by downward deviations from the baseline at approximately 420 °C and 520 °C. According to the caption and related text, the exothermic event near 420 °C corresponds to the ordering and/or texturing of the γ-brass type phase, while the event at ~520 °C marks the formation of the Fe5Si3-type intermetallic phase (space group hP16). The thermogram demonstrates that the 35 h milled powder is thermally stable up to approximately 500 °C, above which phase transformations and silicide precipitation occur upon continued heating. These identified transformation temperatures directly guided subsequent ex-situ annealing experiments at 400 °C, 500 °C, 600 °C, and 800 °C for phase evolution analysis.

Figure 5 presents ex-situ X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the 35 h milled Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high-entropy steel powder annealed at various temperatures. Panel (a) shows the full diffraction profiles at room temperature (R.T.), 400 °C, 500 °C, 600 °C, and 800 °C, with phase markers indicating BCC, γ-brass type, B2-type, and Fe5Si3-type phases. Panel (b) provides an enlarged view of the 42°–46° 2θ range, highlighting subtle peak shifts and asymmetry with temperature. Panels (c–e) display deconvoluted peaks for the 400 °C, 500 °C, and 600 °C samples, respectively, resolving overlapping contributions from BCC, B2-type, and γ-brass type phases around the primary (110) reflection. Panel (f) shows the enlarged 40°–46° region for the 800 °C sample. The as-milled powder remains thermally stable up to 500 °C, retaining the multiphase BCC + γ-brass + B2 assemblage. At 600 °C, diffraction peaks corresponding to the Fe5Si3-type intermetallic phase (hP16) emerge, which the caption correlates with the exothermic event at ~520 °C observed in DTA. By 800 °C, no additional phase transformation occurs, but the fraction of the ordered B2-type phase increases significantly.

This figure presents the X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization of the spark plasma sintered (SPSed) Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high entropy steel processed by mechanical alloying and SPS at 900 °C and 50 MPa. Panel (a) shows the full XRD pattern spanning 2θ = 30°–100°, identifying the BCC (ferrite) phase as the major constituent, alongside partially ordered B2-type, trace γ-brass type, and Fe5Si3-type intermetallic phases, with characteristic reflections such as BCC (110), B2-type (110), and Fe5Si3-type (210) marked. Panel (b) provides a deconvolution of the overlapped peak region between 51° and 54°, resolving individual contributions from BCC, B2-type, and Fe5Si3-type structures. Panel (c) displays the Rietveld refinement result with observed, calculated, background, and difference profiles, along with Bragg position markers for BCC, B2-type, γ-brass type, and Fe5Si3-type phases. Together, these patterns quantitatively confirm the multiphase nature of the sintered alloy and provide the basis for phase fraction and lattice parameter determination reported in the study.

This figure presents microstructural and compositional characterization of spark plasma sintered (SPSed) Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 non-equiatomic high-entropy steel. Panels (a) and (b) show SEM micrographs at different magnifications (scale bars 5 μm and 3 μm, respectively) revealing a multiphase microstructure with distinct contrast regions: light grey (marked 1), white (marked 2), and dark grey/black (marked 3). According to the caption and cited text, these contrasts correspond to BCC (ferrite), partially ordered B2-type, and Fe5Si3-type silicide phases, respectively, along with trace γ-brass type phase; the black and dark grey phases exhibit an onion-type morphology. Panel (c) displays the full-area EDS spectrum confirming the presence of all constituent elements—Fe, Mn, Ni, Al, Si, and C—in the SPSed pellet. The figure collectively demonstrates that the SPS processing at 900 °C and 50 MPa produces a dense multiphase microstructure comprising a BCC + B2 dual-phase matrix with finely distributed nano-sized silicide precipitates, validating the intended alloy composition and phase assemblage.

The figure comprises a backscattered electron (BSE) micrograph and five electron probe microanalysis (EPMA) wavelength-dispersive spectroscopy (WDS) elemental maps (Fe, Mn, Si, Ni, Al) acquired from the same region of the SPSed Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high-entropy steel sample. The BSE image (scale bar: 50 μm) exhibits distinct contrast variations, with marked positions 1 and 2 highlighting different microstructural domains. The Fe, Mn, and Ni maps show relatively homogeneous distributions across the matrix, consistent with the major BCC ferrite phase. The Al map displays localized high-intensity regions that correspond to areas with slightly elevated Fe and Ni contents, which caption states are attributed to a partially ordered B2-type phase. Similarly, Si-enriched spots visible in the Si map indicate Fe-Si-type regions, corroborating the presence of Fe5Si3 silicide precipitates. The γ-brass type phase remains difficult to resolve due to its minimal phase fraction. Together, these maps demonstrate that while the SPSed sample is chemically homogeneous at the macroscale, it possesses a multiphase microstructure that aligns with the XRD-identified phase assembly of BCC, B2-type, trace γ-brass, and Fe5Si3-type phases.

Figure 9 presents the mechanical property characterization and comparative benchmarking of spark plasma sintered (SPSed) Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 low-density high entropy steel. Panel (a) shows the instrumented indentation load versus depth (p-h) curve, reporting an average microhardness of 7.8 ± 0.3 GPa and elastic modulus of 184 ± 5 GPa, with a maximum indentation depth of approximately 5.08 μm reached under 5000 mN. Panel (b) is an optical micrograph of the resulting indent, where the caption states no crack formation is evident even at this peak load. Panel (c) displays the compressive engineering stress-strain curve, indicating an ultimate compressive strength of 2046 ± 160 MPa and a compressive strain of approximately 19%. Panel (d) provides a radar diagram summarizing the integrated physical and mechanical property profile. Panels (e) and (f) compare microhardness versus density and ultimate compressive stress versus density, respectively, benchmarking the present alloy against conventional stainless steels, dual-phase steels, and various reported high entropy alloys. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that this low-density HES achieves markedly superior microhardness and compressive strength at a density near 6.5 g/cm³ relative to many conventional steels and HEA counterparts.

This figure presents a CALPHAD-calculated property diagram showing equilibrium phase amounts versus temperature for the low-density Fe40Mn19Ni15Al15Si10C1 high entropy steel. The horizontal axis spans approximately 500–1500 K and the vertical axis indicates the amount of phases from 0 to 1.2 mol. Six phase fields are plotted: BCC_A2(FE,MN;VA) in red, B2_VACANCY(AL,NI) in blue, LIQUID(FE,MN) in purple, M3SI(MN,FE:SI) in olive, BCC_A2#3(FE;VA) in yellow, and BCC_A2#2(MN;VA,C) in green. At low temperatures, the alloy comprises multiple solid phases including B2 at roughly 0.3 mol, BCC_A2, M3Si silicide, and minor BCC_A2 variants. The M3Si and BCC_A2#3 phases vanish below 600 K, after which BCC_A2 becomes dominant and stabilizes near 0.7 mol between 800 K and 1200 K. The B2 phase decreases gradually with increasing temperature. BCC_A2#2 reappears discontinuously at a trace level near 1100 K. Liquid phase formation initiates near 1200 K and rises sharply, reaching 1.0 mol by approximately 1450 K where all solid phases fully dissolve, indicating complete melting. The diagram illustrates the predicted thermal stability, phase succession, and melting behavior of the alloy.
